content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration · · · Executive/Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                322-5001            322-6441              322 6444

June 15, 1978

78-06-080

Kirk Pessner
Dobbs & Nielsen
Suite 2500, The Alcoa Building
One Maritime Plaza
San Francisco, CA 94111

Dear Kirk:

This will confirm the informal advice that I gave you
by telephone on Tuesday, June 13, 1978.

You described the following situation: The antismoking
initiative will appear on the ballot for California's November, 1978, election. A cigarette manufacturer located outside of California plans to commission a study by an independent institute on the effects of smoke inhaled by nonsmokers.
The manufacturer will not ask that evidence be marshalled to
support a predetermined conclusion, but instead will require
that an objective study be conducted. It is expected that the
study will be completed before the November, 1978, general
election.

The results of the study will determine whether or not it
will be used for political purposes. Thus, if the study
concludes there are harmful effects to nonsmokers, the study
will not be used. However, if the conclusion is that there
are no harmful effects, the study will be utilized as part of
the manufacturer's campaign strategy against the antismoking
initiative. The manufacturer is currently filing campaign
statements on Form 461.

You asked if the cost of the study is reportable and if
it is, when the cost is to be reported. Based on the above
facts, I advised you of the staff's conclusion that the cost
of the study is reportable if an expenditure is made for a
communication that utilizes the study to expressly advocate
the defeat of the antismoking initiative. 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18225. Thus, if the study is commissioned but never
used in connection with the campaign, no reporting obligation
arises. On the other hand, if any part of the study is used

Kirk Pessner
Page Two

June 15, 1978

in connection with communications that expressly advocate the
defeat of the measure, the cost of the study must be reported.

If the cost is reportable, you next ask when it should be
reported. Ordinarily, costs directly traceable to communication are reportable "when the communication is made, or when
payments are made in connection with the development, production
or dissemination of the communication, whichever is earlier."
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(c)(3)(C). Since, as you have
indicated, the results of the study are not predetermined and
no decision has yet been made as to the use of the study, disclosure is not required at the preparation stage. Once a
decision is made to use the study in connection with a campaign
communication, however, disclosure should be made.

I hope this advice is helpful. If you have any questions,
please contact me.

Sincerely,

Barbara
Barbara Campbell
Counsel
Legal Division

State of California

Memorandum

To : Opinion Request Participants                    Date : June 8, 1978


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Barbara Campbell/Barbara

Subject: Reportability of Commissioned Study

     The antismoking initiative will appear on the November, 
1978, ballot. Assume the following: A non-California cigarette 
manufacturer is considering commissioning a study by an independent 
research institute on the effects of smoke inhaled by nonsmokers. 
It is expected that the study will be completed before the November, 
1978, general election. It is not known whether there will be 
competitive bidding for the study.

     The results of the study will determine whether or not 
the study will be used for political purposes. If the study con-
cludes there are harmful effects to nonsmokers, the study will not 
be used. However, if the conclusion is there are no harmful effects, 
the study will be part of the manufacturer's campaign strategy. 
The manufacturer is currently filing Form 461. When and how much 
of the study is reportable? (i.e. when the contract is entered 
into? When the study is completed and determined to be politically 
helpful? When the results are disseminated?)

     In a February 25, 1976, letter to the Federal Energy 
Administration's Administrator (a copy of which is attached), 
Mickey Bennett outlined five facts on which the Commission staff 
relied to conclude FEA's contract with the University of Texas was 
reportable as an expenditure in connection with FEA's opposition 
to Proposition 15.

     Should those same factors be applied to this situation? 
Are there additional factors that should be considered?


BC:plh
Attachment

_____________At the June 12, 1978, Opinion Request meeting, the staff
          concluded that the cost of the study would be reportable 
          if the study were used for a communication that expressly 
          advocated the defeat of the measure. (See letter to Pessner.)

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest

                                                                June 15, 1978

Kirk Pessner
Dobbs & Nielsen
Suite 2500, The Alcoa Building
One Maritime Plaza
San Francisco, CA  94111

Dear Kirk:

     This will confirm the informal advice that I gave you
by telephone on Tuesday, June 13, 1978.

initiative will appear on the ballot for California's November,
1978, election.  A cigarette manufacturer located outside
of California plans to commission a study by an independent institute on the effects of smoke inhaled by nonsmokers.
The manufacturer will not ask that evidence be marshalled to
support a predetermined conclusion, but instead will require
that an objective study be conducted.  It is expected that the
study will be completed before the November, 1978, general
election.

     The results of the study will determine whether or not it
will be used for political purposes.  Thus, if the study
concludes there are harmful effects to nonsmokers, the study
will not be used.  However, if the conclusion is that there
are no harmful effects, the study will be utilized as part of
the manufacturer's campaign strategy against the antismoking
initiative.  The manufacturer is currently filling campaign
statements on Form 461.

     You asked if the cost of the study is reportable and if
it is, when the cost is to be reported.  Based on the above
facts, I advised you of the staff's conclusion that the cost
of the study is reportable if an expenditure is made for a
communication that utilized the study to expressly advocate
the defeat of the antismoking initiative.  2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18225.  Thus, if the study is commissioned but never
used in connection with the campaign, no reporting obligation
arises.  On the other hand, if any part of the study is used

Kirk Pessner
Page Two

June 15, 1978

in connection with communications that expressly advocate the
defeat of the measure, the cost of the study must be reported.

If the cost is reportable, you next ask when it should be
reported. Ordinarily, costs directly traceable to a communication are reportable "when the communication is made, or when
payments are made in connection with the development, production
or dissemination of the communication, whichever is earlier."
1 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(c)(3)(C). Since, as you have
indicated, the results of the study are not predetermined and
no decision has yet been made as to the use of the study, disclosure is not required at the preparation stage. Once a
decision is made to use the study in connection with a campaign
communication, however, disclosure should be made.

I hope this advice is helpful. If you have any questions,
please contact me.

Sincerely,

Barbara
Barbara Campbell
Counsel
Legal Division

Kirk Pessner
Page Two                                    June 15, 1978


in connection with communications that expressly advocate the
defeat of the measure, the cost of the study must be reported

     If the cost is reportable, you next ask when it should be
reported. Ordinarily, costs directly traceable to a communi-
cation are reportable "when the communication is made, or when
payments are made in connection with the development, production
or dissemination of the communication, whichever is earlier."
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(c)(3)(c). Since, as you have
indicated, the results of the study are not predetermined and
no decision has yet been made as to the use of the study, dis-
closure is not required at the preparation stage. Once a
decision is made to use the study in connection with a campaign
communication, however, disclosure should be made.

     I hope this advice is helpful. If you have any questions,
please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara Campbell
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

sections.question: You asked if the cost of the study is reportable and if it is, when the cost is to be reported.
sections.conclusion: Based on the above facts, I advised you of the staff's conclusion that the cost of the study is reportable if an expenditure is made for a communication that utilizes the study to expressly advocate the defeat of the antismoking initiative. Thus, if the study is commissioned but never used in connection with the campaign, no reporting obligation arises. On the other hand, if any part of the study is used in connection with communications that expressly advocate the defeat of the measure, the cost of the study must be reported. Ordinarily, costs directly traceable to a communication are reportable 'when the communication is made, or when payments are made in connection with the development, production or dissemination of the communication, whichever is earlier.' Since, as you have indicated, the results of the study are not predetermined and no decision has yet been made as to the use of the study, disclosure is not required at the preparation stage. Once a decision is made to use the study in connection with a campaign communication, however, disclosure should be made.
sections.question_synthetic: Is a commissioned research study's cost reportable as a campaign expenditure for political purposes, and if so, when must it be reported?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The study cost is reportable only if it is actually used in communications expressly advocating defeat of the initiative; if commissioned but never used, no reporting is required. Disclosure timing depends on when the decision is made to use the study in campaign communications, not when preparation begins.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains some OCR scanning artifacts but maintains clear Q&C structure. Letter dated June 15, 1978, from Barbara Campbell, FPPC Counsel. Contains duplicate page due to document scanning issue but content is consistent between copies.

QUESTION: You asked if the cost of the study is reportable and if it is, when the cost is to be reported.

CONCLUSION: Based on the above facts, I advised you of the staff's conclusion that the cost of the study is reportable if an expenditure is made for a communication that utilizes the study to expressly advocate the defeat of the antismoking initiative. Thus, if the study is commissioned but never used in connection with the campaign, no reporting obligation arises. On the other hand, if any part of the study is used in connection with communications that expressly advocate the defeat of the measure, the cost of the study must be reported. Ordinarily, costs directly traceable to a communication are reportable 'when the communication is made, or when payments are made in connection with the development, production or dissemination of the communication, whichever is earlier.' Since, as you have indicated, the results of the study are not predetermined and no decision has yet been made as to the use of the study, disclosure is not required at the preparation stage. Once a decision is made to use the study in connection with a campaign communication, however, disclosure should be made.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to inquiry about reportability of research study costs commissioned by cigarette manufacturer regarding 1978 California antismoking initiative ballot measure. The letter concludes that costs are reportable only if the study is actually used in campaign advocacy materials.
